macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Position on Food Status and the Principle of Theoretical Possibility

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Rabbi Shimon's view on tum'as ochlin and the reu'uy principle
The case of pigul and whether theoretical possibility of throwing blood makes something food
Rav Ashi's position: redemption by words differs from actions requiring blood-throwing

macro_segments.title: The Challenge from Rabbi Yehoshua's Principle on Me'ilah and Sha'at Heter

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yehoshua's general rule: sha'at heter LaKohanim removes me'ilah status
Definition of sha'at heter: cases of notar, tumah, and yetziah
Clarification: sha'at heter means theoretically possible, not only actual consumption
Gemara's counter-interpretation: the Mishnah discusses cases where blood was actually thrown
Analysis of grammar: past tense in sha'at heter suggests hypothetical rather than actual

macro_segments.title: Rav Ashi's Distinction Between Me'ilah (Sanctity) and Tuma'at Ochlin (Food Status)

micro_segments.title: Me'ilah depends on sanctity level; theoretical possibility of blood-throwing lowers kedushah
Food status requires actual edibility, not mere theoretical possibility
Rashi's explanation: losing a state is easier than gaining a state

macro_segments.title: The Case of Asham Talui and Rabbi Yosei's Application of Theoretical Blood-Throwing

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: one sanctifies an asham in doubt; discovery before shechita nullifies obligation
After shechita with discovery: blood and meat become pasul and must be destroyed
Rabbi Yosei: even if blood is still in a cup, it can be thrown and meat eaten
Rava attributes Rabbi Yosei to Rabbi Shimon: theoretical possibility of throwing makes it valid

macro_segments.title: Alternative Explanation of Rabbi Yosei: Kli Sharet and Weak Psul

micro_segments.title: Gemara's counter: Rabbi Yosei's reasoning may be about kli sharet receiving blood
When blood is caught in kli sharet, it's considered safe despite weak psul

macro_segments.title: Notar and Parah Adumah: Food Status Through Chibat HaKodesh Principle

micro_segments.title: Challenge: notar and parah adumah have obligation to burn, so why are they considered food?
Rav Ashi's answer: chibat HaKodesh in the Mikdash makes things food despite normal rules
Ravina's question: does chibat HaKodesh make them truly tamei or only pasul?
Resolving Reish Lakish's question about dry flour and transmission of tumah
Gemara: chibat HaKodesh operates at d'Rabbanan level for tumah transmission

macro_segments.title: Mishnah: Vows and Sanctification of Mincha with Specific Vessel Types and Quantities

micro_segments.title: Case 1: vow to bring mincha in machavat but brings in marcheshet—valid mincha but unfulfilled vow
Case 2: sanctify flour to bring in specific vessel and use differently—invalid
Case 3: vow two esronot in two vessels but bring in one vessel—valid but unfulfilled
Case 4: sanctify flour for two or one configuration, use differently—invalid
Case 5: vow two esronot in one vessel, bring in two—becomes invalid if reminded of vow

macro_segments.title: Gemara's Analysis: Vow versus Sanctification and the Role of Reminder

micro_segments.title: Why both vessel-type and quantity cases are needed in the Mishnah
Baraita: kli sharet does not sanctify with fixed identity before offering
Abaye: kli sharet does not make kosher for offering but does fix identity as invalid
Distinction: misalignment makes invalid only if fixed at time of neder, not at time of separation
Unresolved question: why does reminder reframe abstract vow into invalid offering?

topical_tags.term: di bei zorik (if you could throw)
